text:
To examine the influence of biomass burning from agricultural waste disposal on free tropospheric chemistry, aerosol samples (TSP) were collected at the summit of Mt. Tai (1534 m a.s.l.) in the North China Plain from May to June 2006. Water-soluble dicarboxylic acids, ketocarboxylic acids and α-dicarbonyls in the aerosol samples were analyzed using GC and GC-MS. The total concentrations of dicarboxylic acids (C2-C11) varied in the range of 220-6070 ng m−3 with oxalic acid (C2) being the major component, while the concentrations of succinic or malonic acid were secondary. A certain number of unsaturated aliphatic diacids and aromatic diacids were also identified, while the sum of $\omega$-oxocarboxylic acids was found to be mainly comprised of glyoxylic acid, and the concentration of pyruvic acid was less than 4.3 ng m−3. The sum of $\alpha$-dicarbonyls was dominated by glyoxal and methylglyoxal.

Aerosol organic carbon (OC) at a mountaintop site in Eastern China was found to be elevated by factors of 4-8 when compared to aerosol OC at ground level in the region's major cities. Diurnal variations of certain organic acids tracked the wheat straw burning season, and peaks in these species coincided with the advection of air masses from the south. These features followed a general pattern of TC and WSOC as demonstrated in previous campaigns. The enhancement of certain species attributed to biomass burning and the oxidation of volatile and semi-volatile VOCs and SVOCs provide compelling explanations for the observed aerosol carbon chemistry in this study. This work demonstrates that seasonal agricultural fires make substantial contributions to organic aerosol levels and hence impact free tropospheric air quality over East China during spring and summer months.
